albums.title: The Bends
albums.reason: Radiohead are your second most-played artist of all time with 2050 plays, but your top tracks skew heavily toward OK Computer and Kid A era — The Bends is the hungry, guitar-driven earlier chapter worth revisiting.

albums.title: Homework
albums.reason: With nearly 3000 all-time plays, Daft Punk are your most-loved artist by a wide margin — Homework is the raw, funky origin point that's easy to overlook once Discovery becomes comfort food.

albums.title: Wish You Were Here
albums.reason: Pink Floyd sit at 1199 all-time plays and this album is their most emotionally direct — a quiet counterpoint to the architectural grandeur of The Wall or Dark Side that rewards a slow revisit.

albums.title: Talkie Walkie
albums.reason: Air clock 1011 all-time plays for you and this is their most intimate, melodically rich record — warmer and more personal than Moon Safari and likely overdue a proper revisit.

albums.title: Melody A.M.
albums.reason: Röyksopp are one of your most-played artists at 1062 plays — this debut is the foundational document of their sound, all downtempo haze and Nordic melancholy that never really ages.

albums.title: Brown Album
albums.reason: Orbital are embedded in your all-time top 12 at 777 plays — this sprawling second album is one of the great overlooked techno records and may not have had a proper spin in years.

albums.title: Room on Fire
albums.reason: The Strokes sit at 652 all-time plays and Room on Fire is their tightest, most underrated album — sharper than Is This It and more consistent than First Impressions, it deserves the vault revisit.

albums.title: Rhythm and Stealth
albums.reason: Leftfield are at 1016 all-time plays and Release the Pressure is one of your most-played tracks ever — Rhythm and Stealth is the heavier, more muscular follow-up to Leftism that often gets skipped.

albums.title: II
albums.reason: You love Radiohead's tension and Daft Punk's electronic craft — Moderat sit exactly at that intersection, and II is their most emotionally devastating record, full of dark texture and melodic ache.

albums.title: Black Sands
albums.reason: Your liked track Kiara by Bonobo is a strong signal toward his catalogue — Black Sands is the breakthrough album where his jazz-inflected, live-instrument downtempo sound fully crystallised.

albums.title: Music Has the Right to Children
albums.reason: Your deep love for Orbital, Leftfield and Röyksopp places you squarely in IDM/downtempo territory — this is arguably the genre's most iconic album and a genuine blind spot if you haven't lived inside it.

albums.title: Things to Make and Do
albums.reason: Your love of Justice, Daft Punk and The Strokes suggests a taste for rhythm with personality — Moloko's masterpiece sits at the crossroads of dance, funk and sharp songwriting in a way few records do.

albums.title: Immunity
albums.reason: You've spent years with Underworld, Orbital and Leftfield — Immunity is the modern heir to that lineage, building from delicate piano into crushing electronic catharsis across a single seamless journey.

albums.title: Dummy
albums.reason: James Blake's soul-damaged electronics and Massive Attack's appearance in your recent listening both point toward trip-hop's founding document — Dummy is still one of the most atmospheric records ever made.

albums.title: Rounds
albums.reason: Four Tet appear in your recent 12-month listening but Rounds predates that and represents a different, more intricate side of his craft — a cult classic in the same lane as your beloved Röyksopp and Air.

albums.title: Swim
albums.reason: Your all-time taste in Pink Floyd's expansiveness and Daft Punk's electronic craft finds a natural modern meeting point here — Swim is krautronic, psychedelic and deeply melodic in equal measure.

albums.title: Man Made Object
albums.reason: Your recent heavy listening to Oscar Peterson Trio and Nils Frahm suggests you're in a jazz-meets-contemporary-classical space — GoGo Penguin sit right there, fusing acoustic jazz trio energy with glitchy electronic architecture.

albums.title: Black Focus
albums.reason: Bill Withers, Charles Bradley and the Oscar Peterson Trio dominate your recent listening — Black Focus merges that jazz soul lineage with UK contemporary energy in a way that feels directly adjacent to where you are now.

albums.title: Persona
albums.reason: Your recent arc through Nils Frahm, Ben Lukas Boysen and Ólafur Arnalds points toward Berlin-school ambient electronics — Rival Consoles works in that same emotional but rhythmically alive space.

albums.title: Elaenia
albums.reason: Your recent listening weaves between Max Cooper's techno, Nils Frahm's minimalism and jazz — Elaenia does all three simultaneously across one stunning, continuous album arc.

albums.title: Choose Your Weapon
albums.reason: Bill Withers and Charles Bradley are central to your recent listening and this is where that soul tradition lands in the 21st century — Hiatus Kaiyote are impossibly musical and warmly human throughout.

albums.title: The Universe Smiles Upon You
albums.reason: Khruangbin appear strongly in your recent and current listening — you've been deep in Con Todo El Mundo but their debut is a different, sparser, more hypnotic world that rewards separate attention.

albums.title: Sleep
albums.reason: Your recent pull toward Brambles, Ólafur Arnalds and Nils Frahm's quieter work all point toward this — Sleep is a monumental eight-hour ambient work, but its single-disc version is a perfect focused listen.

albums.title: Parallel Dimensions
albums.reason: Your recent tracks include deep house and soul-adjacent club music via Roy Davis Jr., DJ Koze and Super Flu — Theo Parrish is the spiritual godfather of that raw, feeling-first deep house lineage.

albums.title: Dehli9
albums.reason: Tosca have 30 plays this week alone and are the single strongest signal in your current listening — Dehli9 is their most richly textured album and hasn't appeared in your recent plays list.

albums.title: Mirage
albums.reason: Ben Lukas Boysen is your second most-played artist this week with 23 plays — Mirage is a different album from the Gravity and Spells you've already been through, continuing his evolution into something more cinematic.

albums.title: Prehension
albums.reason: Joep Beving has 15 plays this month and you've been deep in Liminal — Prehension is his debut and has a rawer, more unguarded quality that pairs perfectly with where your listening head seems to be right now.

albums.title: Waltz for Debby
albums.reason: Bill Evans has 14 plays this month and you've been into New Jazz Conceptions — Waltz for Debby is the warmer, more conversational live counterpart and one of the most beautiful jazz recordings ever made.

albums.title: Geography
albums.reason: Tom Misch has 13 plays this month and you've been spending time with Full Circle — Geography is the debut that established his voice and has a looser, more exploratory energy worth returning to.

albums.title: Smokers Delight
albums.reason: Nightmares on Wax have been present across both your monthly and weekly listening — Smokers Delight is the foundational album of their catalogue and a different trip from In A Space Outta Dub.

albums.title: Starfruit
albums.reason: Moonchild have 7 plays this month and you've been through Voyager — Starfruit is their most sonically lush album and sits beautifully alongside the Bill Withers and Khruangbin warmth you're clearly craving right now.

albums.artist: A Winged Victory for the Sullen
albums.title: Atomos
albums.reason: A Winged Victory for the Sullen have 6 plays this week and you've been in The Undivided Five — Atomos was composed for a Wayne McGregor dance piece and has a more cinematic, spatial quality that matches your current contemplative mood.

albums.title: Mordechai
albums.reason: Khruangbin are one of your strongest signals this week with 12 plays and you've been deep in Con Todo El Mundo — Mordechai is their most vocally present and warmly grooved album, a natural next chapter.

songs.title: Nude
songs.reason: Reckoner is your most-played Radiohead track with 125 plays — Nude is its emotional sibling from In Rainbows, built on the same quiet devastation but rarely mentioned alongside it.

songs.title: Instant Crush
songs.reason: You have six Daft Punk tracks in your all-time top 20 but none from Random Access Memories — Instant Crush is the most emotionally exposed thing they ever made and it deserves a proper revisit.

songs.title: Poor Leno
songs.reason: Röyksopp sit at 1062 all-time plays and this is one of the great deep cuts from Melody A.M. — a slow, aching piece of electronic melancholy that rewards headphone listening after a long gap.

songs.title: Kashmir
songs.reason: Led Zeppelin are at 964 all-time plays and Kashmir is the one track that transcends genre and era entirely — a cyclical, trance-inducing monument that earns its place in any vault revisit.

songs.title: Retrograde
songs.reason: James Blake is your most-played single track artist with Postpone at 156 plays — Retrograde is from Overgrown and achieves a similar emotional scale with more orchestral grandeur.

songs.title: Cowgirl
songs.reason: Underworld are at 633 all-time plays and Born Slippy is your landmark track — Cowgirl is the other side of that coin, slower-building and even more hypnotically rewarding once it opens up.

songs.title: A Day in the Life
songs.reason: The Beatles sit at 831 all-time plays and this is the track that still sounds like nothing else — a vault pick isn't complete without acknowledging their greatest recorded moment.

songs.title: Citizen Erased
songs.reason: Muse are at 712 all-time plays and this is the Origin of Symmetry centrepiece — a seven-minute journey that rewards being turned up loud and suggests your taste runs toward music with genuine structural ambition.

songs.title: Bad Kingdom
songs.reason: Your love of Justice's dance floor drama and Radiohead's emotional weight both point here — Bad Kingdom is the track that crystallises exactly what Moderat do, and it's genuinely stunning.

songs.title: Open Eye Signal
songs.reason: You've lived with Orbital and Underworld for years — Open Eye Signal is the modern equivalent of their peak moments, a fifteen-minute journey that earns every second of its runtime.

songs.title: Glory Box
songs.reason: Your James Blake depth and affection for emotionally heavy electronic music both trace back to something Portishead perfected — Glory Box remains one of the most affecting singles ever recorded.

songs.title: Sun
songs.reason: Your love of Daft Punk's more hypnotic pieces and Pink Floyd's expansive production finds a modern echo here — Sun is the track that made Caribou's Swim make complete sense on first listen.

songs.title: Roygbiv
songs.reason: You've spent thousands of plays in the IDM and downtempo world via Orbital, Röyksopp and Leftfield — Roygbiv is the Boards of Canada track that will immediately confirm why this should be in your life.

songs.title: Sing It Back
songs.reason: Your love of Justice's anthemic dance floor moments and The Killers' melodic drive both find a satisfying answer in Sing It Back — a timeless groove that holds up completely.

songs.title: LesAlpx
songs.reason: Your all-time electronic bedrock artists — Orbital, Underworld, Leftfield — all share a love of long-form hypnotic structure, and LesAlpx is Floating Points doing exactly that with jazz harmony underneath.

songs.title: Moan
songs.reason: You've been loyal to dark, cinematic electronic music for years via Justice, Underworld and Leftfield — Moan is the Trentemøller track that sits perfectly in that lineage with its own distinctly noir character.

songs.title: Raven
songs.reason: Your recent Oscar Peterson Trio and Nils Frahm listening suggests a taste for music that is simultaneously structured and emotionally immediate — Raven is the GoGo Penguin track that makes that case most powerfully.

songs.title: Strings of Light
songs.reason: Your recent listening arc from Bill Withers through Oscar Peterson to Max Cooper suggests a throughline of jazz-inflected feeling — Strings of Light is the track that holds all of that together in one extraordinary groove.

songs.title: Nakamarra
songs.reason: Bill Withers and Charles Bradley are your recent soul anchors — Nakamarra is the track that announced Hiatus Kaiyote to the world and it has that same quality of warmth that feels deeply human.

songs.title: Nespole
songs.reason: Your recent 12-month listening spans Nils Frahm's minimalism and Max Cooper's techno — Nespole sits right at that junction, folding jazz piano into electronic structure with remarkable elegance.

songs.title: Recovery
songs.reason: Your recent Ben Lukas Boysen and Ólafur Arnalds listening reveals a taste for electronics with emotional directness — Recovery is the Rival Consoles track that makes that argument most compellingly.

songs.title: Falling Up
songs.reason: Your recent tracks include Roy Davis Jr., DJ Koze and Super Flu — all roads in that soulful deep house lineage eventually lead to Theo Parrish, and Falling Up is the track that shows you why.

songs.title: On the Nature of Daylight
songs.reason: Your recent listening through Brambles, Hania Rani and Nils Frahm shows a consistent pull toward music that slows time — this is one of the most quietly devastating pieces of modern classical music ever written.

songs.title: Seta
songs.reason: Your recent listening through Dominik Eulberg, Max Cooper and Super Flu places you squarely in the naturalistic, warm-toned end of techno — Stimming is a key figure in that world and Seta is a perfect entry point.

songs.title: Honey
songs.reason: Tosca are your most-played artist this week by a significant margin — Honey is from their album Go, which hasn't appeared in your recent play data, and it captures exactly the hazy, warm quality that's clearly drawing you to them right now.

songs.title: Nocturne
songs.reason: Ben Lukas Boysen is your second most-played artist this week — Nocturne is a deep cut from Gravity that hasn't shown up in your recent tracks and carries the same late-night, eyes-closed quality you're clearly seeking.

songs.title: All Melody
songs.reason: You've been playing Nils Frahm heavily this week across Spaces and Tripping with Nils Frahm — All Melody is the centrepiece title track from the album of the same name and represents a different, more layered side of his work.

songs.title: Maria También
songs.reason: Khruangbin are a strong signal this week and you've been deep in Con Todo El Mundo — María También is from Mordechai and has that same warm, globetrotting groove quality but with a different emotional temperature.

songs.title: Are You Happy
songs.reason: Neal Francis has 12 plays this week and you've been through Return to Zero — Are You Happy is from In Plain Sight, a different album that shows his funkier, more exuberant side and sits well alongside your Bill Withers listening.

songs.artist: A Winged Victory for the Sullen
songs.title: Requiem for the Static King Part One
songs.reason: A Winged Victory for the Sullen have 6 plays this week and you've been in The Undivided Five — this track is from their self-titled debut and has a more stripped, contemplative quality that suits the mood your listening is pointing toward.

songs.title: Peace Piece
songs.reason: Bill Evans has 14 plays this month and your broader listening is clearly drawn toward piano and stillness right now — Peace Piece is a single sustained improvisation that feels like it was made for exactly the mood you're in this week.

songs.title: Get Up
songs.reason: Crazy P are appearing across your recent tracks this week with Changes and We Will F**k You Up — Get Up from the album Stop, Space, Return is a slower, deeper cut that fits the more soulful end of their catalogue.

songs.title: The Man Don't Give a Fuck
songs.reason: Super Furry Animals have 7 plays this week which is a surprise burst of energy in an otherwise contemplative listening week — this is their most unhinged and joyful track and earns its place as a counterweight to everything else on this list.

djSets.setName: HMWL Podcast 430: Niconé & David Hasert (Kompakt / Get Physical / HMWL)
Daphni - FOLD, London. Nov 22, 2025. 7hr DJ set
Klausmarie b2b Alec Troniq - RAVIN' RAVIN' 2026 @ Sektor Evolution
